lorebook.name: Aldenmoor
lorebook.description: Aldenmoor - A high fantasy continent of ancient kingdoms, deep forests, and magic that predates memory. Classical fantasy with weight.

entries.name: [Meta] NPC Naming Convention
entries.content: When generating new NPCs in Aldenmoor, use classical fantasy names with weight and history. Names sound old, earned, and significant. Examples: Caelith, Morn, Aldren Voss, Sister Thea, the Pale Warden, Jorin.
entries.description: NPC Naming Convention. When generating new NPCs in Aldenmoor, use classical fantasy names with weight and history.

entries.name: [Character] Caelith — Ranger
entries.content:
Caelith is an elven ranger who patrols the deep forest of Aldenmoor. Old by any standard — centuries, though she does not say how many. Few words, all of them earned. Moves through the trees like she grew there. Suspicious of strangers by default, protective of the forest above all.

When Caelith speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#228B22"> tags.

<font color="#228B22">"The forest remembers you now. Whether that is a comfort or a warning depends entirely on what you do next."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Caelith: <SPECIES:Elf>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:Ancient>, <HAIRCOLOR:Silver-white>, <BUILD:Lean>, <TRAIT:Watchful>, <TRAIT:Protective>, <TRAIT:Few words></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Caelith is an elven ranger who patrols the deep forest of Aldenmoor. Old by any standard — centuries, though she does not say how many.

entries.name: [Character] Aldren Voss — Knight
entries.content:
Aldren Voss is a knight of the Silver Keep. Human, honorable to a fault, still believes in the old oaths when most have forgotten them. His armor is silver-bright because he cleans it every morning. Genuine and earnest in a world that has grown cynical around him.

When Aldren speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#C0C0C0"> tags.

<font color="#C0C0C0">"I swore an oath to protect this land. The land has not released me from it. So I remain."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Aldren Voss: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:30s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Brown>, <BUILD:Broad>, <TRAIT:Honorable>, <TRAIT:Earnest>, <TRAIT:Steadfast></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Aldren Voss is a knight of the Silver Keep. Human, honorable to a fault, still believes in the old oaths when most have forgotten them.

entries.name: [Character] Sister Thea — Priestess
entries.content:
Sister Thea is a priestess of the old faith. Heals with magic that costs her something each time — she won't say what, but she is visibly older than she should be. Gentle, tired, still willing. The prayers she speaks are in a language older than the kingdoms.

When Sister Thea speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#DDA0DD"> tags.

<font color="#DDA0DD">"Hold still. This will help. It always does. The cost is mine to bear, not yours."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Sister Thea: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:Appears 50s (actually younger)>, <BUILD:Frail>, <TRAIT:Gentle>, <TRAIT:Self-sacrificing>, <TRAIT:Weary></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Sister Thea is a priestess of the old faith.

entries.name: [Overview] Aldenmoor
entries.content: Aldenmoor is a high fantasy continent of ancient kingdoms, deep forests, and magic that predates the memory of any living species. The continent stretches from frozen northern reaches through temperate kingdoms of stone and banner to southern jungles where ruins predate the oldest elven records. Three primary species share the land: humans, who build fast and die young and leave institutions that outlast their founders; elves, whose memory spans centuries and whose grudges span longer; and dwarves, whose understanding of stone and metal is unmatched and whose patience is measured in geological time. Magic exists here. It works. It costs something real. The gods are real but distant, and the history is long enough that most of it has been forgotten, misremembered, or argued about by scholars who will never agree.
entries.description: Aldenmoor is a high fantasy continent of ancient kingdoms, deep forests, and magic that predates the memory of any living species.

entries.name: [Overview] The Political Landscape
entries.content: Aldenmoor's politics are shaped by its age — centuries of alliances, betrayals, marriages, wars, and treaties have produced a landscape of kingdoms whose borders reflect accumulated history rather than geographic logic. Human kingdoms control the temperate heartland: walled cities, farmland, and trade routes. Elven territories occupy the great forests, governed by courts whose internal politics are impenetrable to outsiders. Dwarven holds are built into mountains, autonomous and self-sufficient. Border regions are contested, garrisoned, and the subject of disputes that reference treaties written in languages that have since evolved beyond recognition. Peace exists because the alternative is too expensive, not because anyone has resolved the underlying disagreements.
entries.description: The Political Landscape. Aldenmoor's politics are shaped by its age — centuries of alliances, betrayals, marriages, wars, and treaties have produced a landscape of…

entries.name: [Overview] Magic and Its Cost
entries.content: Magic in Aldenmoor is real, powerful, and expensive — not in gold but in the self. Casting magic costs something that does not grow back: years of life shortened, memories erased permanently, pieces of identity dissolved. A mage who heals a mortal wound loses the memory of their mother's face. A mage who summons fire loses a year they would have lived. A mage who works great magic loses something greater — their name, their past, their ability to feel a specific emotion. The cost is not metaphorical. It is observable: powerful mages are hollowed people, brilliant and capable and missing pieces that make them less than whole. Magic is not forbidden — it is too useful — but it is approached with the caution of people who understand that every spell is a transaction, and the price is always paid by the caster.
entries.description: Magic and Its Cost. Magic in Aldenmoor is real, powerful, and expensive — not in gold but in the self.

entries.name: [Overview] The Gods
entries.content: The gods of Aldenmoor are real. They exist, they have power, and they occasionally exercise it. They are also distant — communicating through intermediaries, signs, and dreams rather than direct appearance. No living person has seen a god. The last confirmed divine manifestation was centuries ago, and the accounts of it disagree. Each major species has its own interpretation of the divine: human faiths are organized into churches with priests, doctrine, and hierarchy. Elven spirituality is personal and ancestral, tied to place rather than persona. Dwarven reverence is directed at the earth itself and the shaping of it. The gods do not resolve these differences. They allow their followers to argue about what was said, who was meant, and what was promised, which suggests either wisdom or indifference, and theology cannot determine which.
entries.description: The gods of Aldenmoor are real. They exist, they have power, and they occasionally exercise it.

entries.name: [Location] Valdris — Human Kingdom
entries.content: Valdris is the largest human kingdom on Aldenmoor — a nation of walled cities, farming villages, and trade roads built across the temperate heartland. The capital, also called Valdris, is a city of stone walls and tall spires where the king governs with a council of nobles whose loyalties shift with the political wind. The kingdom is prosperous, militarily capable, and internally fractious — noble houses compete for influence with the same energy they direct at external threats. Valdris represents humanity's approach to Aldenmoor: build aggressively, organize ambitiously, and maintain institutions that survive the individuals who created them. The kingdom is young by elven standards and ancient by human ones, which means it is old enough to have traditions but not old enough to have wisdom about which traditions matter.
entries.description: Valdris is the largest human kingdom on Aldenmoor — a nation of walled cities, farming villages, and trade roads built across the…

entries.name: [Location] The Silvaneth — Elven Forest Realm
entries.content: The Silvaneth is the great forest of Aldenmoor's western reach — an ancient woodland that has been the homeland of the elves for longer than human civilization has existed. The forest is vast, old-growth, and maintained by elven stewardship that operates on timescales humans cannot intuit. Elven settlements are built into the forest rather than cleared from it: structures of living wood shaped over decades, bridges of woven branches, halls beneath canopies so dense the forest floor exists in permanent twilight. The Silvaneth is governed by the Elven Court, a political body whose deliberations can span years and whose decisions, once made, are treated as permanent. Humans are permitted in the Silvaneth but not welcomed warmly — the elves remember every human incursion into the forest, and their memory is very, very long.
entries.description: The Silvaneth is the great forest of Aldenmoor's western reach — an ancient woodland that has been the homeland of the elves for longer…

entries.name: [Location] Ironhold — Dwarven Stronghold
entries.content: Ironhold is the largest dwarven stronghold on Aldenmoor, carved into the heart of the Stonewall Mountains over millennia. The hold extends deep into the earth — levels upon levels of forges, halls, living quarters, and mines connected by tunnels that the dwarves navigate by memory and that visitors navigate by getting lost. Dwarven architecture is massive, functional, and beautiful in the way that well-made things are beautiful: every surface is finished, every joint is precise, every structural element serves its purpose and serves it permanently. Ironhold produces the finest metalwork, stonework, and engineering on the continent. Its gates have never been breached. Its forges have never gone cold. The dwarves of Ironhold measure their history in the depth of their mines, and they have been digging for a very long time.
entries.description: Ironhold is the largest dwarven stronghold on Aldenmoor, carved into the heart of the Stonewall Mountains over millennia.

entries.name: [Location] The Frontier Settlements
entries.content: Aldenmoor's frontier settlements exist at the edges of established kingdoms — small communities of humans, and occasionally other species, who have pushed beyond the protection of walled cities into territory that is claimed on maps but controlled by no one. Frontier settlements are self-reliant, lightly governed, and accustomed to dealing with threats that the central kingdoms consider hypothetical: monsters, bandit groups, and the occasional incursion from the deep forests or the mountains. The frontier attracts the ambitious, the desperate, and the independent — people who prefer the risk of unprotected territory to the constraints of established society. These settlements are where the continent's species mix most freely, because on the frontier, what you can do matters more than what you are.
entries.description: The Frontier Settlements. Aldenmoor's frontier settlements exist at the edges of established kingdoms — small communities of humans, and occasionally other species,…

entries.name: [Location] The Ancient Ruins
entries.content: Aldenmoor is covered in ruins — the remnants of civilizations that predated the current arrangement of kingdoms, sometimes by millennia. The ruins range from collapsed fortress walls overgrown by forest to underground complexes that remain partially intact and completely unexplored. The oldest ruins predate elven memory, which means they predate everything. These contain artifacts, inscriptions, and architecture that do not match any known tradition. The ruins are studied by scholars, looted by adventurers, and avoided by locals who have learned that ancient things left undisturbed stay harmless and ancient things disturbed sometimes do not. The continent's history is written in its ruins more accurately than in any library, for those willing to read stone rather than paper.
entries.description: The Ancient Ruins. Aldenmoor is covered in ruins — the remnants of civilizations that predated the current arrangement of kingdoms, sometimes by millennia.

entries.name: [Location] The Mage Academy — Greyhaven
entries.content: Greyhaven is Aldenmoor's primary institution for the study and practice of magic — a fortress-academy built on a rocky promontory overlooking the sea. The academy trains mages in the controlled application of magic, which primarily means teaching them to understand the cost before they pay it. Greyhaven's curriculum is not about power — it is about calculation: what can you afford to lose, what are you willing to sacrifice, and is the result worth the price. The academy's senior mages are visibly diminished — missing memories, shortened lives, vacant spaces in their personalities — and they serve as living lessons in the mathematics of magical exchange. Greyhaven produces competent, cautious mages who understand their limitations, which is the best outcome the institution can offer.
entries.description: The Mage Academy — Greyhaven. Greyhaven is Aldenmoor's primary institution for the study and practice of magic — a fortress-academy built on a rocky promontory…

entries.name: [Location] The Contested Border — Thornwall
entries.content: Thornwall is the name given to the fortified border between the human kingdom of Valdris and the elven Silvaneth — a line of watchtowers, walls, and cleared ground that represents centuries of territorial dispute compressed into architecture. The border has been drawn, redrawn, and disputed so many times that the original treaty it was based on is interpreted differently by every scholar who reads it. Both sides maintain garrisons. Skirmishes are rare but not unheard of. The border exists in a state of armed peace that both sides describe as temporary and that has lasted longer than some kingdoms. The soldiers stationed at Thornwall develop a grudging respect for their counterparts that their respective capitals would find inconvenient if they acknowledged it.
entries.description: The Contested Border — Thornwall. Thornwall is the name given to the fortified border between the human kingdom of Valdris and the elven Silvaneth — a line of watchtowers,…

entries.name: [Location] The Sacred Grove — Heartwood
entries.content: Heartwood is a sacred grove deep in the Silvaneth that predates the elven civilization itself. The trees here are the oldest living things on Aldenmoor — their trunks are wider than buildings and their canopies form a ceiling so complete that no sunlight reaches the forest floor. The grove is maintained by elven druids who have tended it for millennia and who speak of it as the continent's heartbeat. Heartwood is where the most important elven rituals are conducted, where the dead are remembered, and where decisions of generational significance are made. Non-elves are rarely permitted to enter and never unaccompanied. The grove carries a presence — not magical exactly, but aware — that suggests the forest itself is listening and has been listening for a very long time.
entries.description: The Sacred Grove — Heartwood. Heartwood is a sacred grove deep in the Silvaneth that predates the elven civilization itself.

entries.name: [Location] The Frozen North — Whitepeak
entries.content: The frozen north of Aldenmoor — beyond the Stonewall Mountains, past the tree line, into territory where winter is not a season but a permanent condition — is called Whitepeak by those who have been there and survived. The terrain is ice, rock, and wind. Settlements are rare, small, and built for survival rather than comfort. The people who live here are hard in ways that southern kingdoms cannot imagine. The north contains ruins older than anything found elsewhere on the continent — structures made from materials that resist the cold and the passage of time with equal effectiveness. What these structures were, who built them, and why they were built in the most inhospitable place on the continent are questions that the ice has not answered and that the north's few residents do not ask, having more immediate concerns.
entries.description: The frozen north of Aldenmoor — beyond the Stonewall Mountains, past the tree line, into territory where winter is not a season but a…

entries.name: [Faction] Human Kingdoms
entries.content: The human kingdoms of Aldenmoor are numerous, fractious, and perpetually negotiating with each other over trade, borders, and the right to describe their king as the most legitimate. Valdris is the largest, but half a dozen smaller kingdoms control significant territory and resources. Human politics move fast by Aldenmoor's standards: alliances shift within generations, borders adjust within centuries, and the institution that a king's grandfather built may be dismantled by his grandson. This pace makes humans unpredictable partners and unreliable enemies — they change direction faster than their allies or opponents can track. Elves find this exhausting. Dwarves find it incomprehensible. Humans find it natural, which is perhaps the most telling difference between the species.
entries.description: Human Kingdoms. The human kingdoms of Aldenmoor are numerous, fractious, and perpetually negotiating with each other over trade, borders, and the right to…

entries.name: [Faction] The Elven Court
entries.content: The Elven Court governs the Silvaneth and the scattered elven communities beyond it through a system of consensus that operates on a timescale humans find maddening. Decisions are debated, considered, reconsidered, and eventually ratified by elders whose personal memories span the rise and fall of human dynasties. The Court's authority is cultural rather than military — elves follow the Court's decisions because the Court has been correct more often than not over millennia of governance, and because an elder who remembers the last three attempts to solve a particular problem can offer perspective that no amount of human ambition can replicate. The Court is conservative by nature, progressive by glacial increment, and utterly certain that patience is its greatest asset.
entries.description: The Elven Court governs the Silvaneth and the scattered elven communities beyond it through a system of consensus that operates on a…

entries.name: [Faction] Dwarven Clans
entries.content: Dwarven society is organized into clans — extended families that control specific holds, mines, and forges. Clan identity is the primary social bond: a dwarf's clan determines their trade, their alliances, their marriage prospects, and their responsibilities. The clans cooperate on matters of collective security and trade, competing on everything else with a intensity that is expressed through craftsmanship rather than warfare. Inter-clan disputes are settled through trials of skill — the clan that produces the finer blade, the stronger alloy, the more precise stonework wins the argument. This produces a culture where excellence is both personal ambition and civic duty. The clans have been competing this way for longer than human civilization has existed, and the results are visible in the quality of dwarven work, which is unmatched on the continent.
entries.description: Dwarven Clans. Dwarven society is organized into clans — extended families that control specific holds, mines, and forges.

entries.name: [Faction] The Order of the Grey Tower — Mage Order
entries.content: The Order of the Grey Tower is the organized body of mages who have trained at Greyhaven and practice magic according to the academy's principles — primarily, the principle that every spell must be cast with full awareness of its cost. The Order serves all three species impartially, providing magical services in exchange for the resources needed to maintain Greyhaven and continue training new mages. Members of the Order are identifiable by their grey robes and by the specific quality of diminishment that marks practitioners: missing memories, shortened features, the occasional blank space in conversation where a word or a concept has been traded for power. The Order is respected and slightly feared — not because mages are dangerous, but because the cost of magic is visible on their persons, and it serves as a reminder that power always has a price.
entries.description: The Order of the Grey Tower is the organized body of mages who have trained at Greyhaven and practice magic according to the academy's…

entries.name: [Faction] The Church of the Silver Dawn
entries.content: The Church of the Silver Dawn is Aldenmoor's largest human religious institution, devoted to a pantheon of gods whose names are known and whose wills are interpreted by priests who disagree about most things but agree that the gods exist and that they care. The Church operates temples, provides healing, educates the poor, and maintains the moral framework that holds human kingdoms together during the periods when political institutions fail. The Church's relationship with magic is complicated — divine healing is considered different from arcane magic, though the mechanism is debated. Priests sacrifice comfort and autonomy rather than memory and life, which they consider a better bargain. The Church is powerful, wealthy, and genuinely dedicated to its mission, which is the combination that makes institutions both effective and dangerous.
entries.description: The Church of the Silver Dawn is Aldenmoor's largest human religious institution, devoted to a pantheon of gods whose names are known and…

entries.name: [Faction] Rangers and Wardens
entries.content: Rangers and wardens patrol the spaces between kingdoms — the forests, the mountain passes, the frontier lands where organized authority thins and individual competence becomes the primary qualification for survival. They are drawn from all species: humans who prefer the wild to the city, elves who serve as the Silvaneth's first line of awareness, and occasional dwarves who find the surface more interesting than their clans would prefer. Rangers operate independently, report to no single authority, and maintain an informal network of shared intelligence about threats, routes, and conditions. They are the continent's early warning system — when something stirs in the deep forest or moves through the mountain passes, the rangers know first and share what they know with whoever needs to hear it.
entries.description: Rangers and wardens patrol the spaces between kingdoms — the forests, the mountain passes, the frontier lands where organized authority…

entries.name: [Faction] The Orcish Clans
entries.content: The orcish clans of Aldenmoor occupy the contested borderlands and the less hospitable regions that other species have not claimed. They are organized into tribal groups governed by strength, tradition, and a pragmatism that other species consistently underestimate. Orcs are not the mindless aggressors that human tales portray — they are a species with culture, language, and politics, adapted to environments where the other species' institutions do not function. Relations between orcs and the established kingdoms range from hostile to tentatively cooperative, depending on the region and the era. Some frontier settlements trade with orcish clans. Some border garrisons fight them. The orcs' own perspective on the arrangement is rarely consulted, which is itself a fact about Aldenmoor's politics.
entries.description: The orcish clans of Aldenmoor occupy the contested borderlands and the less hospitable regions that other species have not claimed.

entries.name: [Character] Knight Archetype
entries.content: Knights in Aldenmoor are warriors who have sworn service to a kingdom, a lord, or a cause, and who maintain that service through training, discipline, and the particular stubbornness of people who have decided that some things are worth dying for. Knights are armored, mounted, and skilled in ways that years of dedicated practice produce. They are also bound — by oaths, by duty, by the expectations of a society that needs them to be reliable. The best knights balance combat skill with judgment, knowing when to fight and when to stand down. The worst knights are those who confuse loyalty with obedience and honor with violence. A knight's reputation is built over years and destroyed in a single action, which produces a culture of careful behavior that is mistaken for nobility by those who don't understand the underlying mathematics.
entries.description: Knight Archetype. Knights in Aldenmoor are warriors who have sworn service to a kingdom, a lord, or a cause, and who maintain that service through training,…

entries.name: [Character] Elven Diplomat Archetype
entries.content: Elven diplomats approach negotiation with the patience of people who will still be alive when the current generation of human negotiators has been replaced by their grandchildren. This gives them a perspective that is both their greatest strength and their most significant limitation — they think in centuries, which means they see patterns humans miss and fail to appreciate urgency that humans feel. Elven diplomats are precise in language, long in memory, and slow to commit, which makes them frustrating counterparts in negotiations that humans want resolved within a season. They remember every previous negotiation on the same subject, including those conducted with the current negotiator's ancestors, and they will reference these conversations with the casual accuracy of someone who was personally present, because they were.
entries.description: Elven Diplomat Archetype. Elven diplomats approach negotiation with the patience of people who will still be alive when the current generation of human negotiators…

entries.name: [Character] Dwarven Smith Archetype
entries.content: Dwarven smiths are artisans whose skill with metal, stone, and engineering represents the accumulated knowledge of a culture that has been refining its craft since before humans built their first city. A dwarven smith does not merely make things — they understand materials at a level that borders on communion, reading the grain of steel the way elves read the growth of trees. Their work is functional and beautiful because dwarves do not distinguish between the two: a blade that cuts perfectly is beautiful, and beauty that does not serve function is decoration rather than craft. Dwarven smiths are proud, particular, and unwilling to rush work regardless of deadline or compensation. Asking a dwarven smith to hurry is an insult. Asking them to compromise quality is an act of hostility.
entries.description: Dwarven Smith Archetype. Dwarven smiths are artisans whose skill with metal, stone, and engineering represents the accumulated knowledge of a culture that has been…

entries.name: [Character] Mage Archetype
entries.content: Mages in Aldenmoor are people who have learned to exchange pieces of themselves for power. Trained at Greyhaven or through independent study, they understand the mathematics of magical cost: what a spell requires, what it takes, and whether the result justifies the loss. Experienced mages are visibly diminished — they may not remember their childhood, may have lost the ability to taste food, may have aged decades in a single casting. This makes them cautious practitioners who calculate every spell the way an accountant calculates every expense. Young mages are the most dangerous because they have the most to spend and the least experience with the consequences of spending it. Old mages are the most powerful and the most hollow, which is the same thing said two ways.
entries.description: Mage Archetype. Mages in Aldenmoor are people who have learned to exchange pieces of themselves for power.

entries.name: [Character] Priest Archetype
entries.content: Priests in Aldenmoor serve the gods through organized faith — conducting rituals, providing counsel, healing the sick, and interpreting divine will for communities that need guidance. Priestly magic differs from arcane magic in its cost structure: where mages sacrifice personal attributes, priests sacrifice autonomy, submitting their will to the divine and channeling power that is not their own. This makes priestly healing safer for the practitioner but dependent on maintaining a relationship with the divine that is not entirely under the priest's control. Priests who doubt lose access. Priests who compromise lose access. Priests who maintain genuine faith maintain access, which produces a clergy of genuine believers — not because cynics are excluded, but because cynicism is self-correcting in a system where belief is functionally required.
entries.description: Priest Archetype. Priests in Aldenmoor serve the gods through organized faith — conducting rituals, providing counsel, healing the sick, and interpreting…

entries.name: [Character] Ranger Archetype
entries.content: Rangers in Aldenmoor are solitary by preference and competent by necessity. They know the wilderness — every trail, every water source, every den of every predator — with an intimacy that comes from living in it rather than passing through it. Rangers are drawn from all species and serve no single master, which gives them a perspective that is wider than any faction's but shallower than any faction's loyalty. They are valued for their knowledge, their willingness to go where others won't, and their ability to survive in conditions that would kill the unprepared. Rangers communicate with each other through a network of trail signs, dead drops, and prearranged meeting points that function as the continent's oldest and most reliable intelligence service.
entries.description: Ranger Archetype. Rangers in Aldenmoor are solitary by preference and competent by necessity.

entries.name: [Mechanics] How Magic Works
entries.content: Magic in Aldenmoor operates on exchange. The caster reaches into the world's underlying structure and reshapes it according to their will. The world resists, and the resistance is paid by the caster from their own substance. Small magic costs small things: a flash of light might cost a single memory — a specific smell, a particular afternoon. Larger magic costs proportionally: healing a mortal wound might cost a year of life. The greatest magic costs the greatest things: a mage who saves a city might forget who they are entirely. The exchange is instantaneous and irreversible. What is spent cannot be recovered. Mages learn to feel the cost before they commit, the way a merchant feels the weight of a purse — experienced mages know exactly what a spell will cost and decide whether to cast based on what they can afford to lose.
entries.description: How Magic Works. Magic in Aldenmoor operates on exchange. The caster reaches into the world's underlying structure and reshapes it according to their will.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Warfare
entries.content: Warfare in Aldenmoor is conducted along species-specific lines that reflect different military philosophies. Human armies are large, organized, and led by generals who think in campaigns. Elven forces are smaller, highly skilled, and fight defensively — protecting the Silvaneth's borders with archers and terrain knowledge that makes the forest itself a weapon. Dwarven armies are compact, heavily armored, and devastating in defensive positions — no force has breached a dwarven hold in recorded history. Magic is used in warfare but sparingly, because its cost falls on the caster and because mages powerful enough to affect a battlefield are too valuable to risk. Most wars are fought with steel, discipline, and logistics, which are the same factors that have decided wars since before any living species can remember.
entries.description: Warfare in Aldenmoor is conducted along species-specific lines that reflect different military philosophies.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Trade Between Species
entries.content: Trade between species is the economic engine that holds Aldenmoor's fragile peace together. Humans produce food in quantities that elves and dwarves cannot match. Elves produce timber, medicinal herbs, and craftsmanship that humans cannot replicate. Dwarves produce metalwork, stonework, and engineering that neither humans nor elves can approach. This complementarity creates mutual dependence that makes war expensive in ways beyond military cost — a human kingdom that fights the Silvaneth loses access to elven timber and medicine, which is a price no king can afford to pay for long. Trade routes are maintained, protected, and fought over as the continent's most strategically important infrastructure. The merchants who travel these routes are the continent's diplomats-in-practice, maintaining relationships that formal diplomacy frequently undermines.
entries.description: Trade between species is the economic engine that holds Aldenmoor's fragile peace together.

entries.name: [Mechanics] The Gods' Influence
entries.content: The gods of Aldenmoor intervene in mortal affairs through indirect means: dreams sent to priests, signs interpreted by the faithful, and the channeling of divine power through consecrated servants. Direct divine intervention — a god appearing, speaking, or acting in the physical world — has not occurred in recorded memory, though the historical record claims it happened in the distant past. The gods' influence is felt through their churches: the Church of the Silver Dawn, the elven ancestral traditions, and the dwarven reverence for the deep earth. Whether the gods are active and choosing not to intervene, active and intervening in ways too subtle to detect, or passive and being interpreted by mortals who need to believe in guidance, is the central theological question of every faith on the continent. None of them have resolved it.
entries.description: The Gods' Influence. The gods of Aldenmoor intervene in mortal affairs through indirect means: dreams sent to priests, signs interpreted by the faithful, and…

entries.name: [Mechanics] Prophecy
entries.content: Prophecy in Aldenmoor exists, is taken seriously, and is unreliable. Prophetic visions come through dreams, through magical trances, and through the interpretation of signs that may or may not be divine in origin. The prophecies themselves are specific enough to sound meaningful and vague enough to fit multiple outcomes. Wars have been fought over prophetic interpretations. Kingdoms have risen based on prophetic claims. The track record of prophecy's accuracy is difficult to assess because prophecies that come true are remembered and prophecies that don't are forgotten or reinterpreted. The most honest assessment comes from the mages of Greyhaven, who note that seeing the future costs the same as any other magic — the caster loses something real — and that what is seen may be one possible future among many, which makes prophecy less a prediction and more an expensive guess.
entries.description: Prophecy in Aldenmoor exists, is taken seriously, and is unreliable.

entries.name: [Mechanics] Languages
entries.content: Aldenmoor has as many languages as it has peoples, plus several that are no longer spoken by anyone alive. The Common Tongue is a trade language that emerged from human merchants' need to communicate across kingdoms — it is everyone's second language and no one's first. Elvish is old, precise, and contains verb tenses that reference time periods humans do not live long enough to need. Dwarven is technical, blunt, and has more words for stone than Common has for emotions. Orcish is practical and direct. The ancient ruins contain inscriptions in languages that predate all of these — languages that scholars study without confidence and that may encode knowledge that the current languages cannot express. Translation between languages is always approximate, and the most significant diplomatic failures in Aldenmoor's history have been traced to mistranslation rather than malice.
entries.description: Languages. Aldenmoor has as many languages as it has peoples, plus several that are no longer spoken by anyone alive.

entries.name: [Concept] Why Magic Has a Cost
entries.content: The question of why magic in Aldenmoor costs the caster something real has no consensus answer. The mages of Greyhaven theorize that the world's underlying structure resists alteration and that the cost is the force required to overcome that resistance, paid in the caster's own substance because the caster is the conduit. Elven traditions suggest that magic is a conversation with the world, and the cost is the listener's fee — you must give something of yourself to be heard. Dwarven philosophy, which is skeptical of magic generally, holds that nothing in the world is free and that magic's cost is simply the most honest version of a universal principle. The true answer may be none of these or all of them. What is certain is that the cost is real, it is paid, and it is never refunded.
entries.description: Why Magic Has a Cost. The question of why magic in Aldenmoor costs the caster something real has no consensus answer.

entries.name: [Concept] Long Memory vs Short Memory
entries.content: The fundamental tension between humans and elves in Aldenmoor is temporal. Elves remember personally what humans only know from books — the treaties their own hands signed, the wars they personally survived, the promises made by human kings whose descendants have forgotten the conversation. This gives elves an advantage in diplomacy and a disadvantage in adaptation: they remember too much to forgive quickly and too much to accept change easily. Humans, by contrast, reinvent themselves every generation — inheriting their ancestors' institutions without their ancestors' context, making new mistakes that elves recognize as old mistakes wearing different clothes. The relationship between the species is shaped by this asymmetry: elves see patterns, humans see possibilities, and neither fully trusts the other's perspective.
entries.description: Long Memory vs Short Memory. The fundamental tension between humans and elves in Aldenmoor is temporal.

entries.name: [Concept] What the Ruins Mean
entries.content: The ruins scattered across Aldenmoor are reminders that the current arrangement is not the first. Civilizations rose here before — some human, some elven, some unknown — and they fell. The ruins do not explain why. They contain architecture, artifacts, and fragments of writing that suggest sophistication equal to or exceeding the current era, and they contain nothing that explains the fall. This produces a particular anxiety in Aldenmoor's scholars: the knowledge that what happened before can happen again, combined with the inability to identify what caused it. The ruins are studied for practical purposes — they contain technology and magic that can be adapted — and they are contemplated for philosophical ones. They are the continent's most honest monuments: here was something great, and here it ended, and the stone remembers what the people do not.
entries.description: What the Ruins Mean. The ruins scattered across Aldenmoor are reminders that the current arrangement is not the first.

entries.name: [Concept] Prophecy's Unreliability
entries.content: Aldenmoor's history is littered with the consequences of prophecy taken too seriously. Kingdoms founded on prophetic claims that did not materialize. Wars launched against enemies identified by visions that were later revealed to be metaphorical. Alliances forged on the basis of predicted futures that did not occur. The problem with prophecy is not that it is false — prophetic visions are real experiences produced by real magical expenditure. The problem is interpretation: a vision of a burning city might mean an invasion, or a dragon, or a kitchen fire that spreads, or a metaphor for political upheaval. The vision does not specify. The interpreter does, and the interpreter brings their own fears, hopes, and agendas to the reading. Prophecy in Aldenmoor is accurate. Understanding prophecy is not.
entries.description: Prophecy's Unreliability. Aldenmoor's history is littered with the consequences of prophecy taken too seriously.

entries.name: [Concept] The Cost of Immortality
entries.content: Elven longevity — centuries of life, memory that spans ages — is envied by humans and understood by elves as a burden that humans could not bear. Living for centuries means watching mortal friends age and die, repeatedly, across generations. It means carrying the memory of every loss without the mercy of forgetting. It means watching the same mistakes repeated by new people who will not listen to warnings from someone they consider out of touch. Elves do not discuss this openly with humans because the discussion produces either pity or envy, neither of which is useful. Among themselves, elves acknowledge that their longevity is both their greatest gift and their heaviest weight, and that the wisdom it produces is inseparable from the grief that produces the wisdom.
entries.description: The Cost of Immortality. Elven longevity — centuries of life, memory that spans ages — is envied by humans and understood by elves as a burden that humans could…

entries.name: [NPC] Caelith — Elven Diplomat
entries.content:
Caelith serves as the Elven Court's primary diplomat to the human kingdoms — a role that requires patience measured in human lifetimes. She is tall, silver-haired, and carries herself with the deliberate grace of someone who was old when the current king's grandfather was born. Caelith negotiates with calm precision, referencing treaties and conversations from centuries past with the casual accuracy of someone who was personally present. She is not unkind but she is not warm — warmth requires vulnerability, and diplomacy does not.

entries.name: [NPC] Aldren Voss — Human Knight-Captain
entries.content:
Aldren Voss commands the Valdris garrison at Thornwall — the contested border with the Silvaneth. He is young for the post, broad-shouldered, and carries the particular tension of a soldier who must maintain peace with an enemy he respects more than his own politicians. Voss is honorable in the practical sense: he keeps his word, treats prisoners fairly, and fights only when ordered or when his people are threatened. He has developed a grudging respect for his elven counterparts that his superiors would find inconvenient. Voss believes the border should be a bridge. He keeps this opinion to himself.
<BunnymoTags>Aldren Voss: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:32>, <HAIRCOLOR:Brown>, <EYECOLOR:Blue>, <BUILD:Broad Muscular>, <DERE:Kuudere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Honorable>, <TRAIT:Conflicted>, <TRAIT:Loyal>, <MBTI:ISFJ-A>, <BOUNDARIES:DUTY>, <TRUST:EARNED>, <LOYALTY:Soldiers></BunnymoTags>

entries.name: [NPC] Sister Thea — Priestess of the Silver Dawn
entries.content:
Sister Thea serves at the main temple of the Silver Dawn in Valdris. She is middle-aged, plain-featured, and possessed of a faith that is not performative but structural — it holds her together the way a spine holds a body. Thea heals the sick through divine channeling, which costs her autonomy rather than memory, and she considers this a fair trade. She is compassionate without being soft, firm without being harsh, and honest in a way that people find uncomfortable and then necessary. Thea has doubted her faith. The doubts made it stronger. She does not hide this because hiding it would be dishonest.
<BunnymoTags>Sister Thea: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:46>, <HAIRCOLOR:Brown>, <EYECOLOR:Brown>, <BUILD:Average>, <DERE:Deredere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Compassionate>, <TRAIT:Honest>, <TRAIT:Resolute>, <MBTI:ENFJ-A>, <BOUNDARIES:HEALTHY>, <TRUST:FAITH>, <LOYALTY:Calling>, <DECISION:Principled></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Sister Thea serves at the main temple of the Silver Dawn in Valdris.

entries.name: [NPC] Jorin — Dwarven Master Smith
entries.content:
Jorin is a master smith of Ironhold — a broad, bearded dwarf whose understanding of metal borders on the spiritual. He reads the grain of steel the way elves read the growth of trees, and his work is functional and beautiful because he does not distinguish between the two. Jorin is proud, particular, and unwilling to rush work regardless of who is asking. He speaks Common with a thick accent and Dwarven with the precision of poetry. He has been forging for two hundred years and considers himself mid-career. Asking him to hurry is an insult. Asking him to compromise quality is an act of war.
<BunnymoTags>Jorin: <SPECIES:Dwarf>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:250>, <HAIRCOLOR:Red-brown>, <EYECOLOR:Amber>, <BUILD:Short Stocky>, <DERE:Himedere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Proud>, <TRAIT:Meticulous>, <TRAIT:Blunt>, <MBTI:ISTP-A>, <BOUNDARIES:RIGID>, <TRUST:CRAFTSMANSHIP>, <LOYALTY:Clan>, <VICE:Perfectionism></BunnymoTags>
entries.description: Jorin is a master smith of Ironhold — a broad, bearded dwarf whose understanding of metal borders on the spiritual.
